title: Hi-C Contact Maps and TAD Triangles
subtitle: A Hi-C map turns 3D genome folding into a heatmap of contact frequency along a chromosome.
summary: The HiC-TAD project visualizes chromatin contacts, TAD architecture, boundaries, polymer models, and deletion effects.

externalSources.label: PMC: TAD boundaries and regulatory territories

artifact.title: Contact intensity around a movable boundary
artifact.description: Shift the boundary and watch contact intensity reorganize into two domains.

sections.heading: What the Matrix Means
body: A Hi-C contact map is a square matrix. Each row and column represents a genomic bin, and each cell records how often two bins were observed near each other in 3D space.
The diagonal is strong because nearby genomic positions often contact each other. Off-diagonal blocks reveal longer-range folding structure.

sections.heading: Why TADs Look Like Triangles
body: A topologically associating domain is a region whose internal loci interact more frequently with each other than with neighboring regions.
When plotted as an upper triangle, the high-contact square block becomes a triangle. Boundaries show up where the triangle pattern changes.

rows: A domain with enriched internal contacts

sections.heading: Connection to Deletion Analysis
body: The project uses wild-type and deletion predictions to ask whether removing a boundary or enhancer-like sequence changes the contact map.
That makes the heatmap more than a visualization: it becomes a structural hypothesis about gene regulation.

pitfalls: Reading every bright cell as a direct physical contact between two fixed points.
Ignoring resolution; a 5 kb map and a 100 kb map reveal different structures.
Treating TAD boundaries as perfectly sharp walls.

quiz.question: What does a bright off-diagonal block usually suggest?
options: Bins in that region contact more often than expected
The bins are always adjacent in sequence
quiz.explanation: Brightness represents contact enrichment between two genomic regions or within a domain.
